    Home › Aircraft › Boeing B-52 Stratofortress

    Boeing B-52 Stratofortress

    Boeing · Strategic bomber · In service since 1955 — planned to 2050s

    The hundred-year aircraft.

    Length48.5 m
    Wingspan56.4 m
    Range14,080 km
    MTOW220 t
    Built744
    First flight1952
    Our numbers

    Derived metrics

    Computed by aircraft.fyi from the core specification — see how we calculate these.

    Range per tonne#32 of 65
    64.1 km/t maximum range ÷ MTOW in tonnes
    Span-to-length ratio#9 of 68
    1.16 wingspan ÷ fuselage length
    Weight per metre#16 of 68
    4.53 t/m MTOW ÷ fuselage length
    Range in hours#10 of 65
    13.4 h maximum range ÷ cruise speed
    Sense of scale

    How big is it, really?

    The Boeing B-52 Stratofortress's wingspan, against things you already know the size of.

    Boeing B-52 Stratofortress56.4 m
    Football pitch (width)68 m
    Blue whale25 m
    New Routemaster bus11.2 m
    You (probably)1.75 m
    #34 of 68 by length → #21 by weight → #21 by speed →
    Specification

    The numbers

    Length48.50 m
    Wingspan56.40 m
    Height12.40 m
    MTOW219,600 kg
    Range (unrefuelled)≈14,080 km
    Weapons load≈31,750 kg
    Engines8 × PW TF33 (RR F130 coming)
    First flight15 April 1952
    Built744 (last in 1962)

    Compare it

    Put the Boeing B-52 next to something

    vs Rockwell B-1B Lancer →vs Tupolev Tu-160 →
    vs Tupolev Tu-160 vs Boeing C-17 Compare with anything →
    ← PreviousBoeing 787 Dreamliner Next →Boeing C-17 Globemaster III

    Last verified: 2026-07-14 · Spot an error? business@luck.fyi

    The story

    What makes it different

    The B-52 first flew in 1952, and the US Air Force currently plans to fly it into the 2050s — a service life of a full century, unmatched by any combat aircraft in history. Crews today fly the same airframes their grandfathers flew; the last one was built in 1962.

    Eight engines hang in four twin pods under a drooping 56.4-metre wing so flexible the outriggers at its tips keep it off the tarmac. Across seven decades it has been continually re-equipped and re-tasked — few airframes in history have been reinvented so many times.

    76 B-52Hs remain, and rather than retire them the USAF is fitting new Rolls-Royce F130 engines and new radars to create the B-52J. The joke in Air Force circles is that the last B-52 pilot has not been born yet — and it may not be a joke.

    Common questions

    Boeing B-52 Stratofortress — the short answers

    How fast is the Boeing B-52 Stratofortress?
    The Boeing B-52 Stratofortress cruises at about 1,047 km/h — roughly 565 knots, or 651 mph.
    How far can the Boeing B-52 Stratofortress fly?
    The Boeing B-52 Stratofortress has a maximum range of about 14,080 km (7,603 nautical miles, 8,751 miles). At its cruise speed that is roughly 13.4 hours in the air.
    Is the Boeing B-52 Stratofortress still in production?
    Status: In service since 1955 — planned to 2050s. The Boeing B-52 Stratofortress first flew in 1952, and about 744 have been built.
    How big is the Boeing B-52 Stratofortress?
    The Boeing B-52 Stratofortress is 48.5 m long with a wingspan of 56.4 m and a height of 12.4 m. Its maximum takeoff weight is 219,600 kg.
    Variants

    The Boeing B-52 Stratofortress line

    B-52A–G
    The Cold War generations — all retired.
    B-52H
    The 1961–62 survivors — today's entire fleet.
    B-52J
    The re-engined modernisation now underway.
    Operators

    Who flies the Boeing B-52 Stratofortress

    All 1 current operators, verified 2026-07-14. Fleet counts are approximate where marked ≈.

    USUS Air ForceThe sole operator — Global Strike Command, Reserve and test fleets, serving toward a 100-year career.76

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
